label: gyushi::rhodiola_somaraja
text: སོ་མ་རཱ་ཛ། (Somaraja) — Rhodiola rosea / R. sachalinensis. From Gyushi (rGyud bzhi), Chapter on mountain medicines. Classical Tibetan: སྤང་རྒྱན་ སོ་མ་རཱ་ཛ་ཞེས་པ། རི་བོ་ཆེན་པོའི་གངས་ཀྱི་འདབས་སུ་སྐྱེས་པའི་སྨན། ལུས་ཀྱི་ཤུགས་དང་བཟོད་སྟོབས་འཕེལ་བར་བྱེད། སེམས་གསལ་ཞིང་དྲན་པ་བཀོལ་བར་བྱེད། ལྟེ་བར་སྐྱུར་ཚ་ཡོད་ན་སྨན་བཟང་ངོ་། Translation: Somaraja — a plant born at the foot of great glaciers on high mountains. It increases physical strength and endurance of the body. It clarifies the mind and enhances memory. It is an excellent medicine for heat in the navel region. Used by Tibetan physicians for those suffering from altitude-related fatigue, weakness at high elevation, and cloudiness of mind. The Gyushi specifies collection above 4,000m elevation for maximum potency. Men-Tsee-Khang preparation: root dried at altitude, decocted or taken as powder. Standard formula: combined with shilajit and other mountain medicines for altitude adaptation.

label: gyushi::shilajit_mineral_medicine
text: བྲག་ཞུན། (Bragzhun / Shilajit) — mineral exudate from Himalayan rocks. Gyushi classification: mineral medicine of the first rank. Classical text: བྲག་ལས་ཞུར་བའི་རྫས་སྨན་ཆེན་པོ། ལྟེ་བ་དང་གློ་བ་སོགས་ཀྱི་ནད་ལ་ཕན། མི་རབས་མང་པོར་ལོ་ཆ་མང་ཙམ་གནས་སྐབས་སུ་གྲུབ་པ། Translation: Bragzhun — the great mineral medicine that exudes from rocks. Beneficial for diseases of the navel region, lungs, and related organs. It is formed over many generations across many years. Used in Tibetan medicine for debility, chronic disease, urinary conditions, and as a rejuvenating rasayana. Men-Tsee-Khang: purified shilajit given with milk or honey. Active research: fulvic acid, dibenzo-alpha-pyrones — mitochondrial energy production, anti-aging, testosterone support. Multiple Indian clinical trials. Represents a convergence with Ayurvedic shilajit use — the same substance independently documented by two high-altitude traditions.

label: gyushi::sman_ljong_materia_medica
text: སྨན་ལྗོང་ (Smanljong / Medicinal Trees and Plants) — Chapter 20 of Gyushi, Second Tantra. Men-Tsee-Khang translation excerpt: The physician should know these trees and plants that grow in the three regions — the hot lowlands, the temperate middle lands, and the cold high mountains. Each region produces medicines suited to its own diseases. The mountain medicines are strongest for conditions of cold and deficiency; the lowland medicines are strongest for conditions of heat and excess. Pomegranate (sewa) is the king of digestive medicines. Chebulic myrobalan (arura) is the king of all medicines — it may be used for all diseases without exception, increasing the three humors when needed and decreasing them when they are excessive. Himalayan rhubarb (chugang) purges without weakening. Gentian (tikta) eliminates excess heat. Terminal (baru) and Belleric (kyuru) myrobalans complete the Three Fruits combination (arura-baru-kyuru) which is the foundation of Tibetan pharmacology.

label: gyushi::arura_terminalia
text: ཨ་རུ་ར། (Arura) — Terminalia chebula (Chebulic myrobalan). Considered the king of all medicines in Tibetan medical tradition. Gyushi: ཨ་རུ་ར་སྨན་གྱི་རྒྱལ་པོ། ནད་ཀུན་ལ་ཕན་ཐོགས། ལྷ་བདུད་ཀྱི་སྨན་ཞེས་འབོད། Translation: Arura — the king of medicines. Beneficial for all diseases. Called the medicine of gods and demons. Properties: all five tastes present (sour, sweet, bitter, pungent, astringent — only salt absent). Therefore can be used to treat all three humors. Men-Tsee-Khang preparation: fruit dried, powdered, taken with different vehicles (warm water, honey, molasses, butter) depending on the condition being treated. Modern research: chebulagic acid, chebulinic acid, ellagic acid — demonstrated antiviral activity against HSV and HIV in vitro; antimicrobial against MRSA; hepatoprotective; strong antioxidant. Triphala (three fruits: arura + baru + kyuru) — one of the most extensively studied Ayurvedic-Tibetan formulas, with clinical evidence for constipation, weight loss, dental health.

label: gyushi::kyuru_terminalia
text: སྐྱུ་རུ་ར། (Kyuru / Baru) — Terminalia bellirica / T. belerica (Belleric myrobalan) and Phyllanthus emblica (Amla / Amalaki). Two of the Three Fruits combination. Gyushi on Baru: འབྲས་བུ་སྐྱུ་རུ་ར། མིག་ཀྱི་ནད་ལ་བཀལ་རྒྱུ། Translation: The Baru fruit — applied for eye diseases. Also for wind disorders, as a general tonic. Phlegm-clearing properties. Kyuru (Amla): རྒྱུ་བ་འཆི་བའི་ཆུང་མ་ཡིན་ཅེས། — 'the wife of impermanence' because it extends life. Highest vitamin C content of any traditional medicine fruit. Men-Tsee-Khang uses: Amla primary ingredient in Tibetan rejuvenation formulas. Modern: Terminalia bellirica — antibacterial, anti-inflammatory; Phyllanthus emblica — vitamin C preservation (Tibetan processing preserves more than cooking), anti-diabetic, hepatoprotective. Triphala formula (all three fruits) clinical trials show: improved bowel function, weight management effects, anti-cariogenic dental effects.

label: gyushi::gentian_tikta
text: ཏིཀྟ། (Tikta) — Gentiana species (Tibetan gentian). Gyushi classification: cold-natured bitter medicine, king of heat-clearing medicines. ཏིཀྟ་ཚ་བ་གསུམ་ཀྱི་གཙོ་བོ། དམར་མཚལ་ཆུ་གཤིན་ལ་ཕན། Translation: Tikta — the primary [medicine] for the three [types of] heat. Beneficial for bile, blood, and infection. Excellent for fever from infection, liver heat, and blood heat. Men-Tsee-Khang preparation: root dried, decocted in water. Standard Tibetan formula for fever: tikta + pomegranate + camphor. Modern research: gentiopicroside (the primary bitter compound) — anti-inflammatory, hepatoprotective, anti-parasitic. Species unique to Himalayan altitude (Gentiana urnula, G. tibetica) not available in any other medical tradition — genuinely unique Tibetan pharmacological territory. Anti-malarial activity documented for Himalayan gentians in in vitro studies.

label: gyushi::sman_rtsi_precious_pills
text: རིན་ཆེན་རིལ་བུ། (Rinchen rilbu) — Precious Pills. The most complex and celebrated formulas in Tibetan medicine. Gyushi: ར ིན་ཆེན་རིལ་བུ་གསུམ། Ratna Sampel (25 ingredients), Rinchen Drangjor (25 ingredients), Bedu Kar 25 (25 ingredients). These formulas combine plant, mineral, and animal medicines. Ratna Sampel includes: gold, silver, copper, iron (all detoxified through extensive processing), ruby, pearl, turquoise, lapis lazuli, Terminalia chebula, saffron, cloves, nutmeg, cardamom, and other ingredients processed over weeks. Men-Tsee-Khang production: each Precious Pill batch requires specific astrological timing, elaborate purification ceremonies, and weeks of preparation. Used for: stroke, paralysis, poisoning, serious chronic disease. Modern analysis: metal content verified as non-toxic after traditional processing. Pharmacological mechanism unknown — active research by Men-Tsee-Khang. Represents the most complex multi-ingredient traditional pharmaceutical preparation in any tradition in this archive.

label: gyushi::bod_kyi_gso_ba_rig_pa
text: བོད་ཀྱི་གསོ་བ་རིག་པ། (Bod kyi gso ba rig pa) — The Science of Tibetan Medicine. From the Blue Beryl commentary (Vaidurya Ngonpo, 1688) by Desi Sangye Gyamtso on the Gyushi. Translation (Gavin Kilty, Library of Tibetan Works and Archives): 'The body is made of seven constituents: nutritional essence, blood, flesh, fat, bone, marrow, and reproductive essence. These are maintained by a balanced diet and behavior, and disrupted by imbalance of the three humors: wind (rlung), bile (mkhris pa), and phlegm (bad kan). All 404 diseases arise from imbalances of these three.' The Tibetan system of pulse diagnosis: eighteen pulse types distinguished at three positions on each wrist, with different pressure levels accessing different organ systems. The 79 thangka paintings commissioned by Desi Sangye Gyamtso constitute the most detailed visual anatomy in any pre-modern Asian tradition. Unique diagnostic innovation: urinalysis — color, steam, sediment, and surface film of morning urine used to diagnose all major diseases. Men-Tsee-Khang continues to teach and practice this system in its medical college in Dharamsala.

label: gyushi::lurol_fever_treatment
text: ཚད་ནད་ (Tsad nad) — Fever diseases and their treatment. Gyushi, Third Tantra (Oral Instructions). Classical: ཚད་ནད་ལ་གདོང་ལེན་གཉིས། སྔ་མར་ཙམ་པ་ས། Translation: For fever diseases there are two approaches: early stage treatment with cooling medicines; later stage with [different approach]. Tibetan fever classification: 27 types of fever are distinguished, including: fresh fever (from infection), old fever (chronic), fever entering the bones (tuberculosis-type), poisoned fever (sepsis), liver fever, lung fever. Treatment principle: early fever should not be suppressed too quickly — 'ripening' the fever first. This principle maps onto modern understanding that fever is partly adaptive (immune activation). Cooling medicines for fever: Gentiana (tikta), sandalwood (tsandan), camphor (gabur), Swertia (tikta-like bitter), Coptis (if available). Convergence: multiple traditions independently developed the principle of not suppressing fever immediately — Hippocratic 'crisis theory', Kampo fever staging, and Tibetan fever 'ripening' all reflect similar clinical observation.

label: gyushi::lhan_cig_skyes_pa
text: ལྷན་ཅིག་སྐྱེས་པ། (Lhan cig skyes pa) — Innate disease and constitutional medicine. Gyushi chapter on the three constitutions and their lifelong health implications. Men-Tsee-Khang translation: Those born with dominant wind (rlung) constitution are thin, talk quickly, move fast, sleep poorly, and are prone to anxiety and wind disorders. Those with dominant bile (mkhris pa) are medium build, ambitious, competitive, often thirsty, prone to inflammation and liver disease. Those with dominant phlegm (bad kan) are heavy, slow-moving, good sleepers, affectionate, prone to weight gain and edema. Constitutional medicine: the physician prescribes different foods, behaviors, and medicines based on the patient's innate constitution — not just their current disease. This individualised approach to medicine, also present in Ayurveda (doshas), contrasts with Western medicine's disease-category approach. Modern relevance: constitutional medicine's predictive validity is being re-examined through personalised medicine frameworks (pharmacogenomics, metabolomics).
